headline: The Lions of Mesopotamia Face Football's Modern Empire
subheadline: Iraq return to the tournament after 40 years of absence and walk straight into a date with two-time champions France
openingNarrative:
Forty years. That is how long Iraq have waited to hear the tournament anthem echo through a stadium before one of their matches. The last time they competed at this level, the year was 1986, the venue was Mexico, and the world was a fundamentally different place. In the decades since, Iraqi football has endured war, sanctions, unimaginable hardship, and a period where simply fielding a national team required an act of courage that most nations will never understand.

And then, in 2007, something miraculous happened. Against every conceivable odd, Iraq won the Asian Cup — beating Saudi Arabia in the final while bombs fell on Baghdad. The celebrations that followed were not merely about football. They were about the survival of hope itself. That tournament remains one of the most emotionally powerful stories in the history of sport.

Now Iraq are back at the tournament, and their reward for qualifying is a match against a France side that has never been more loaded with talent or more motivated to win. This is Didier Deschamps' final tournament as manager, with Zinedine Zidane waiting to take the reins. Deschamps has guided France to three of the last four tournament finals. He wants to leave with the trophy.

The squad he has at his disposal is evolving and yet terrifyingly deep. Antoine Griezmann's retirement from international football has been absorbed seamlessly — Michael Olise has stepped into the creative void with the audacity of a player who won best player at the 2024 Olympics and chose France over England without hesitation. The Tchouameni-Camavinga double pivot, both forged at Real Madrid, gives France a midfield that blends power and finesse. And Kylian Mbappe, fully fit and entering the tournament on 56 international goals — one short of Thierry Henry's all-time record — is the most dangerous striker on the planet.

France's March form underlines the depth of this squad: a 2-1 victory over Brazil and a 3-1 win against Colombia in friendlies played on American soil. At +550, they are the bookmakers' second favorite to win the tournament. Iraq qualified through AFC qualifying with 3 wins, 4 draws, and 3 losses, scoring 9 goals and conceding 10. Those are modest numbers. But they represent something immeasurable: the return of a footballing nation to its rightful stage.

For 90 minutes, Iraq will stand on the same pitch as Mbappe, Olise, and Tchouameni. The result may be predictable. The emotions will be anything but.

tacticalBreakdown.title: The Fortress Under Siege
tacticalBreakdown.narrative:
Iraq under Jesus Casas play a technically proficient 4-2-3-1 that emphasizes possession and creative midfield play. In AFC qualifying, this approach produced neat football and competitive results. Against France, however, the reality will be different. Iraq will need to sacrifice their natural inclination to play on the front foot and instead adopt a deep, compact defensive shape.

Expect a 4-5-1 or 5-4-1 from Iraq, with two banks of players sitting narrow and deep. Mohanad Ali, Iraq's most talented attacker, will be asked to hold up the ball and create counter-attacking opportunities from isolated positions. Aymen Hussein, the top scorer in qualifying with 3 goals, will look to exploit any set-piece opportunities.

France's 4-2-3-1 will probe patiently. The Tchouameni-Camavinga double pivot will dictate the tempo, recycling possession and waiting for the moment to accelerate. Mbappe will drift across the front line, looking for the half-second of space that is all he needs. Olise, operating in the creative pocket behind the striker, will look to unlock Iraq's deep block with the kind of incisive passing and dribbling that low-block defenses struggle to contain. The full-backs — Theo Hernandez marauding from the left, Kounde overlapping from the right — will push high to provide width and stretch Iraq's compact shape.

Iraq's best tactical weapon is discipline. If they can maintain their defensive shape for 90 minutes and avoid being drawn into individual duels they cannot win, they can limit France's opportunities. The moment they lose concentration, France will strike.
tacticalBreakdown.keyMatchup: Iraq's defensive discipline vs Mbappe's movement — 90 minutes of concentration against the world's most dangerous attacker

dataInsights.title: By The Numbers
dataInsights.eloAnalysis: France (ELO: 1870, ranked 3rd) vs Iraq (ELO: 1450, ranked 65th) — a 420-point chasm that represents one of the tournament's most extreme quality differentials. France's win probability exceeds 88% by ELO calculation.
dataInsights.formAnalysis: France qualified with 5 wins and 1 draw, scoring 16 and conceding 4, with Mbappe contributing 4 goals. They followed that with March 2026 friendlies against Brazil (2-1 win) and Colombia (3-1 win) on American soil, scoring five goals in two matches. Iraq's AFC qualifying campaign was a battle: 3 wins, 4 draws, 3 losses, 9 scored and 10 conceded. The goal difference deficit tells the story of a team that fought for every point.
dataInsights.squadValueComparison: France ($1.64B) vs Iraq ($30M) — the financial gulf is staggering. Mbappe's individual market value ($225M) is 7.5 times the value of Iraq's entire 26-man squad. France's substitutes bench alone would outvalue most of the tournament's squads.
dataInsights.injuryImpact: France can rotate their entire starting XI and still field a team capable of winning most matches at this tournament. Mbappe is fully fit; Saliba carries a minor ankle concern but has Upamecano and Konate as world-class alternatives. Iraq need their best players — particularly Mohanad Ali and Aymen Hussein — at full fitness to have any attacking threat.
dataInsights.bettingPerspective: France at +550 are the tournament's second favorites, and this is one of their most lopsided individual fixtures. Iraq's realistic hope is keeping the scoreline respectable. A draw would rank among the most significant upsets in tournament history. An Iraq victory would be unprecedented in its scale.
dataInsights.historicalPattern: These teams have met only once, with France winning. Iraq's last tournament in 1986 saw them lose all three group matches. The 2007 Asian Cup triumph, however, proved Iraq are capable of performing above their level when the emotional stakes are highest.

playerSpotlights.name: Kylian Mbappe
playerSpotlights.narrative: The living definition of unfair advantage — and a man on the threshold of history. Mbappe's 56 international goals leave him one short of Thierry Henry's all-time French record, and a match against Iraq is the kind of fixture where personal milestones meet team objectives. Fully fit after his knee recovery and sharp after scoring against Brazil in March, his combination of pace, power, technique, and finishing makes him the most complete forward in world football. His move to Real Madrid has added a layer of tactical sophistication to his game — he now dictates matches rather than merely devastating defenses. Against Iraq, he will be the sword that cuts through whatever defensive wall they construct.
playerSpotlights.statline: 56 international goals (1 short of all-time French record), hat-trick in the 2022 tournament final, France captain and Real Madrid's talisman

playerSpotlights.name: Mohanad Ali
playerSpotlights.narrative: Iraq's most talented attacker carries the hopes of 40 million people. Named Asian Young Footballer of the Year in 2018, Mohanad Ali has the technical quality to compete at this level — his pace, his directness, and his willingness to take on defenders give Iraq an attacking dimension they desperately need. He will be isolated for long periods, running into cul-de-sacs of French defenders, but one moment of brilliance could write his name into Iraqi football history forever.
playerSpotlights.statline: Iraq's most valuable player at $5M; 3 goals in tournament qualifying; 2018 Asian Young Player of the Year

playerSpotlights.name: Aurelien Tchouameni
playerSpotlights.narrative: France's midfield anchor provides the platform from which all their attacking magic flows. Alongside Camavinga in the double pivot that has become the heartbeat of Deschamps' system, Tchouameni's positioning, his passing range, and his ability to read the game allow France to control matches without ever appearing to strain. Against Iraq, his composure will set the tempo — dictating when France probe patiently and when they accelerate into the spaces behind Iraq's defense. The Tchouameni-Camavinga partnership, forged at Real Madrid, gives France a midfield foundation that most nations can only dream of.
playerSpotlights.statline: Real Madrid's midfield general; one half of France's double pivot controlling tempo and territory

playerSpotlights.name: Aymen Hussein
playerSpotlights.narrative: Iraq's top scorer in qualifying embodies the fighting spirit that has defined Iraqi football through its darkest years. Hussein is a penalty-box predator who can finish with both feet and his head. He will see precious few chances against France's defense, but his clinical instincts mean that any opportunity — a set piece, a deflected clearance, a rare counter-attack — could produce a goal that would mean more to his nation than most will ever understand.
playerSpotlights.statline: Iraq's leading qualifier scorer with 3 goals; clinical finisher and national team talisman

theVerdict.title: The Verdict
theVerdict.narrative:
The cold reality of this match is that France have the quality to win comfortably. Their depth, their individual brilliance, and their tournament experience place them in a different category to Iraq. Mbappe will find space. Olise will create chances. The Tchouameni-Camavinga pivot will control the midfield. And France's defense, even with Saliba's ankle being monitored, will rarely be threatened.

Deschamps, in his final tournament, will treat every match as an opportunity to build momentum. The March victories over Brazil and Colombia showed a France team that is not just talented but hungry — a squad that has absorbed Griezmann's retirement, integrated Olise's creativity, and emerged stronger for it. Against Iraq, that hunger will be directed at making a statement.

But the warm reality — the human reality — is that this match means something far beyond the scoreline. For Iraq, stepping onto a tournament pitch after 40 years of absence is a triumph in itself. Their fans will create an atmosphere that transcends the football, and their players will fight for every ball as if their lives depend on it.

France will win professionally and comprehensively. But Iraq will compete with dignity, and if Mohanad Ali or Aymen Hussein can find one moment of magic — one goal that makes 40 million Iraqis erupt in celebration — then the scoreline becomes irrelevant.

The tournament is not always about winning. Sometimes, it is about returning.

alternativeScenarios.reasoning: Iraq produce a heroic defensive display reminiscent of their 2007 Asian Cup grit. They keep France at bay for most of the match before conceding late. The result earns Iraq enormous respect and gives them momentum for their remaining group matches.

The greatest shock of the group stage. Iraq score first from a set piece, and France's frustration grows as Iraq defend with 10 men behind the ball. France equalize late but cannot find a winner. Iraq celebrate a draw as if they have won the tournament.

closingLine: Iraq have waited 40 years for this moment. France may win the match. But Iraq have already won something far more important: the right to be here, on football's grandest stage, representing a nation that refused to let its footballing dream die.
